result.text:
What made Iran bold enough to strike Israel directly?

The answer is Trump. Tehran may not field a first-class air force or navy, but they possess the absolute best detection equipment in the world for one specific signal: a lack of willingness to fight. For years, Hamas and Hezbollah detected this exact hesitation in Israel and exploited it to gain ground. Today, Iran is doing the exact same thing to the United States. It comes down to the old idiom: give them an inch, and they’ll start firing at a mile of your sovereign territory.

Two months of projected American weakness and a blind obsession with securing a deal have brought us straight to this moment. The collapse started when Washington chose to look the other way during Iranian missile and drone launches in the Gulf. It accelerated every time the U.S. conceded ground just to keep Tehran from abandoning the talks, culminating in the immense pressure to hold Israel back from striking Beirut and the forcing of a hasty, fragile “ceasefire” in southern Lebanon. Tehran smells the desperation and knows exactly what the U.S. is terrified of losing. As an advisor to Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei phrased it: “In the bombing in Beirut, the enemy set the negotiating table on fire for the third time.”

So far, it seems Iran guessed correctly. Overnight, Trump went on Fox News with a message for Tehran: “You’ve shot your missiles, that’s enough, get back to the table and make a deal.” In so many words, Trump is simply repackaging Joe Biden’s infamous April 2024 warning to Iran: “Don’t.” Judging by the multiple, separate ballistic barrages launched from Iranian territory this morning, it promises to be just as effective.
....

result.text:
🚨 Russia is preparing to significantly expand Mi-8 helicopter production. According to documents obtained by Dallas Analytics, the Kazan Helicopter Plant is scheduled to manufacture 72 Mi-8MTV-1 helicopters in 2026–2027 under Russia’s state defence order.
❗This figure is nearly twice as high as previous estimates of Russia’s production capacity and could allow Moscow to offset wartime losses sustained during its invasion of Ukraine. If achieved, these production targets could help restore Russia’s airborne assault and transport aviation capabilities to levels approaching those seen before the full-scale invasion.
📁 At the same time, the leaked documents reveal persistent weaknesses within Russia’s defence-industrial base, including dependence on engine and avionics suppliers, financing challenges, production bottlenecks, and growing reliance on state reserve funds to sustain military output.
What threat could these helicopters pose to Ukraine and NATO? How realistic are Russia’s ambitious production targets? And what do the documents reveal about the vulnerabilities of the Russian defence industry?
🔍 Read our full investigation: https://t.co/bpegXvN6cZ 
We have also expanded the list of our channels – you can now follow Dallas Analytics on Substack, Threads, LinkedIn and Instagram for further investigations and updates.

data.preview_translation: In recent days, a report presented by Konstantin Malofeev and Alexander Dugin on the main threats to Russia in the second quarter of the 21st century has generated significant buzz. Numerous comments on X in particular have emerged, pointing to what this report is and where it
data.translation:
In recent days, a report presented by Konstantin Malofeev and Alexander Dugin on the main threats to Russia in the second quarter of the 21st century has generated significant buzz. Numerous comments on X in particular have emerged, pointing to what this report is and where it leads. Some argue that nuclear weapons represent an opening move aimed at seizing the initiative.

The report was almost uncritically embraced as a long-term strategy for the Russian Federation. Meanwhile, the situation is far more complex. There is no evidence to suggest that either Dugin or Malofeev belongs to the innermost decision-making circle. I would rather say that the report reveals the mindset of certain Russian imperial-conservative circles, rather than the entire system.

Additionally, the report itself may have been a media provocation, an ideological maneuver, or an attempt to spark debate. By contrast, the conclusion that because nuclear weapons appear in the scenario, Russian elites have deemed their use inevitable is far too sweeping.

The report was immediately labeled a strategic projection, overlooking its propaganda function. From my perspective, the report could just as easily have been a tool for mobilizing elites, an instrument of factional infighting, a political signal, or an element of strategic intimidation, given the waning effectiveness of Russian operations in Ukraine.

Finally, it is worth recalling a certain historical lesson. During the Cold War, some Western analysts interpreted the structure of the Soviet Union's armed forces, operational plans, and preparations for nuclear weapon use as evidence that Moscow was pursuing a surprise first nuclear strike. Meanwhile, post-Cold War interviews with Soviet military personnel painted a far more nuanced picture. They confirmed the existence of plans for preemptive nuclear use, exercises involving a rapid transition to nuclear war, and preparations for counterforce strikes. At the same time, they maintained that these were not preparations to initiate war, but to wage it in a scenario where political leadership deemed conflict unavoidable.

In other words, in Soviet thinking, the distinction boiled down to two different questions: "strike first if war is inevitable" and "strike first to start that war." For many Soviet officers, this was a fundamental difference that, in their view, was often misunderstood in the West. The Russians did not plan actions toward the latter option.

This does not mean, of course, that contemporary Russia should be analyzed through the lens of the Soviet Union. It does, however, illustrate how easily analysts can leap from the observation that a state is preparing certain courses of action to the conclusion that it has already decided to implement them. In my assessment, a similar risk of overinterpretation exists in the case of the Malofeev and Dugin report as well.

W ostatnich dniach dużym echem odbił się raport przedstawiony przez Konstantina Małofiejewa oraz Aleksandra Dugina dotyczący głównych zagrożeń dla Rosji w drugiej ćwiartce XXI wieku. Zwłaszcza na X pojawiły się liczne komentarze wskazujące, czym jest ten raport i do czego prowadzi. Niektórzy są zdania, że broń nuklearna jest ruchem otwierającym, którego celem jest przejęcie inicjatywy.

Raport został przyjęty niemal bezkrytycznie jako długoterminowa strategia FR. Tymczasem sytuacja jest bardziej skomplikowana. Nie ma żadnych dowodów wskazujących na to, że zarówno Dugin, jak i  Małofiejew należą do ścisłego kręgu decyzyjnego. Powiedziałbym raczej, że raport pokazuje sposób myślenia części rosyjskich środowisk imperialno-konserwatywnych, a nie całego systemu. 

Dodatkowo sam raport mógł być prowokacją medialną, zabiegiem ideologicznym lub próbą wywołania debaty. Natomiast wniosek, że ponieważ broń jądrowa pojawia się w scenariuszu, elity rosyjskie uznały jej użycie za przesądzone, jest zbyt daleko idący.

Raportowi od razu przylepiono łatkę projekcji strategicznej, pomijając jego funkcję propagandową. Z mojej strony równie dobrze raport mógł być narzędziem mobilizacji elit, instrumentem walki frakcji, sygnałem politycznym czy elementem ostraszania strategicznego, biorąc pod uwagę słabnące efekty rosyjskich działań na Ukrainie.  

Na koniec warto przypomnieć pewną lekcję historyczną. W czasie zimnej wojny część zachodnich analityków interpretowała strukturę sił zbrojnych ZSRR, plany operacyjne oraz przygotowania do użycia broni jądrowej jako dowód na to, że Moskwa dąży do przeprowadzenia zaskakującego pierwszego uderzenia nuklearnego. Tymczasem z wywiadów przeprowadzonych po zakończeniu zimnej wojny z radzieckimi wojskowymi wynikał znacznie bardziej złożony obraz. Potwierdzali oni istnienie planów wyprzedzającego użycia broni jądrowej, ćwiczeń związanych z szybkim przejściem do wojny nuklearnej oraz przygotowań do uderzeń kontrsiłowych (counterforce strike) . Jednocześnie twierdzili, że nie były to przygotowania do rozpoczęcia wojny, lecz do jej prowadzenia w sytuacji, gdy kierownictwo polityczne uzna konflikt za nieunikniony.

Innymi słowy, w radzieckim rozumieniu różnica sprowadzała się do dwóch odmiennych pytań: „uderzyć pierwszym, jeśli wojna jest nieunikniona” oraz „uderzyć pierwszym, aby tę wojnę rozpocząć”. Dla wielu radzieckich oficerów była to fundamentalna różnica, która, ich zdaniem, często była źle rozumiana na Zachodzie. Rosjanie nie planowali działań w kierunku tej drugiej opcji. 

Nie oznacza to oczywiście, że współczesną Rosję należy analizować przez pryzmat Związku Radzieckiego. Pokazuje jednak, jak łatwo analitycy mogą przejść od stwierdzenia, że państwo przygotowuje określone opcje działania, do wniosku, że podjęło już decyzję o ich realizacji. W mojej ocenie podobne ryzyko nadinterpretacji występuje również w przypadku raportu Małofiejewa i Dugina.

result.text:
As part of President @ZelenskyyUa’s visit to the United Kingdom, I was pleased to hold a bilateral meeting with my British colleague @YvetteCooperMP and thank her for the hospitality.

We discussed the outcomes of the President’s important visit to the UK and further steps to put our leaders’ agreements into action.

I informed Secretary Cooper about the battlefield dynamic, Ukraine’s successful long-range sanctions against Russia, and the achievements of our defense industry.

We spoke in detail about peace efforts and the role the United Kingdom and Europe more broadly.

I thanked my colleague for sharing the outcomes of her recent visits to China and India. We discussed their important contributions to advancing peace.

We also coordinated next steps in increasing pressure on the aggressor. 

I am grateful to Secretary Cooper for reaffirming the UK’s willingness to further ramp up economic pressure on the Russian regime.

Together, we are defending our shared values and advancing a safer and more prosperous Europe.

legacy.full_text:
I’ve been in Finland watching a NATO drill as troops from across the alliance were worked on resisting a Russian invasion. But while there, the struggle of operating a tank in a 21st century battlefield was made very clear. Here’s an extract of my story:

Hidden in dense woodland

result.text:
I’ve been in Finland watching a NATO drill as troops from across the alliance were worked on resisting a Russian invasion. But while there, the struggle of operating a tank in a 21st century battlefield was made very clear. Here’s an extract of my story:

Hidden in dense woodland 18 miles from the Russian border, Nato anti-tank teams lie in wait to ambush a Finnish column.

The scrape of metal tracks across a dirt road pierces the silent forest as the pack of German-made Leopard 2 tanks charges forward.

Without warning, camouflaged soldiers break cover from the treeline and unleash a volley of missiles at the heavily armoured vehicles before the Finnish conscripts have time to react.

Within seconds, three tanks are destroyedand their young crews, aged 19 and 20, are killed as the anti-tank teams quietly slip back into the wilderness.

Almost 300 miles to the south, in the lake-potted forests near Kouvola, another group of Leopard 2s is immobilised by drones and artillery strikes on the border, having bunched up too closely together on a track through pine trees.

Both attacks were simulated by Nato forces as part of a major military exercise called Northern Star involving 5,000 troops from seven nations, including Britain, in Finland’s heavily wooded eastern Kainuu region.

But the drills reveal what could be Nato’s reality in a future war against Russia if the alliance doesn’t move away from traditional arms such as tanks and towards modern warfare. Last week Latvia’s military chief warned that Moscow has gained an edge over Nato in drone warfare and could exploit Europe’s slow rearmament drive and invade the Baltics by 2028.

Nato commanders now face a looming crisis: learning how to fight on a 21st-century battlefield dominated by AI-assisted drones that can rapidly spot and target tanks and military vehicles from the skies.

The alliance must now keep up with the evolution of land warfare to be able to fight Russia, military chiefs told The Telegraph. If it doesn’t, there are fears we could see the fall of the Baltics to Moscow.
That will mean speeding up the procurement of drones and the development of factories to build them at scale, while pumping more money into developing the next generation of military vehicles with integrated autonomous systems.

Traditionally, the frontlines were ruled by tanks. Acting as armoured juggernauts, they were able to punch through enemy defences with firepower.

And while military planners still believe tanks have a place in war, some have questioned whether they are able to hold ground as they did in the past.

Full story: https://t.co/we1SaZaYth

legacy.full_text:
Seeing some tweets about how Iran has imposed a new deterrence equation on Israel and the region. That seems like a whole lot of motivated reasoning.

First, let's be clear about how much of a reversal this represents for Iran's strategic doctrine: instead of its Arab proxies

result.text:
Seeing some tweets about how Iran has imposed a new deterrence equation on Israel and the region. That seems like a whole lot of motivated reasoning.

First, let's be clear about how much of a reversal this represents for Iran's strategic doctrine: instead of its Arab proxies protecting Iran from attack, Iran is now trying to bail them out—and inviting attacks on its own territory as a result.

Second, in four past rounds of fighting (going back to April 2024), Iran was unable to deter Israel because its missile barrages failed to cause strategic damage. It could not impose costs significant enough to change Israeli behavior.

Indeed, as @NicoleGrajewski and others have written, those attacks arguably invited further conflict rather than deterred it. The salvos overnight do not seem any different. Iran's act of "deterrence" wound up causing more damage to Iran than to the country it sought to deter.

The tools that have been more successful over the past few months—at compellence, rather than deterrence—have been the ones aimed at Gulf states and global energy markets. But to use those tools now (or in the future) risks collapsing the entire ceasefire.

The "equation" here relies on Donald Trump to restrain Israel. It doesn't seem viable at all in the long term, unless Iran is prepared to risk endless escalatory cycles that would ultimately be very damaging for Iran itself.

result.text:
Russian propagandist Olezhich has fled Russia and is asking for US help.

A former active participant in propaganda broadcasts and an "expert" on anti-Western shows, Viktor Olezhich, left Russia after pressure from the FSB.

According to him, the security forces came to his home twice - in December and at the beginning of February - with direct threats. The reason was allegedly his refusal to cooperate with the FSB and his unwillingness to obtain Russian citizenship.

Olezhich stated that despite having "information sensitive to the state", the pressure continued and became systematic.

He is currently in Greece and has appealed for help to the US State Department.

Previously, Olezhich regularly appeared on Russian propaganda programs "Time Will Tell" and "Meeting Place", and also positioned himself as an expert of the Center for Current Politics, known for his harsh criticism of the "Russophobic West".

JD Vance took time this week to explain to the British that the murder of 18-year-old student Henry Nowak was actually about immigration.

Justice Secretary David Lammy apparently rang Vance to set the record straight. “You’re wrong about this,” Lammy told him. Which must have been an uncomfortable conversation for a vice-president who has built an entire political career on being confidently incorrect about things happening in other people’s countries.

Meanwhile, across the Atlantic, England’s football squad was busy preparing for the World Cup in Kansas City, Missouri. Nine people were shot near England’s planned training facility and hotel on Troost Avenue at four in the morning. ￼Police arrived to find a large crowd scattering. ￼A second shooting the same evening, just miles away on Troost Avenue, left two people dead. ￼

The Kansas City Police were quick to reassure everyone that the incident “did not occur near a World Cup venue or anything else World Cup-related.” ￼ Which is technically true, in the same way that a house fire next door isn’t technically in your living room.

The Americans are hosting a World Cup. They have strong opinions about immigration and British crime statistics. They would just like everyone to ignore the bit where nine people get shot near the visiting team’s hotel before breakfast.

Gandalv / @Microinteracti1

result.text:
The EU’s sanctions policy regarding Russian oil exports is a monumental and embarrassing failure.

After 21 sanctions packages—packed with grand rhetoric, solemn press conferences, and endless lists of "sanctioned vessels"—Russia is exporting an average of 3.46 million barrels of crude oil per day by sea in May 2026. That is 120,000 barrels more than in the entirety of 2025 and higher than any annual average since the 2022 invasion. Bloomberg tanker data does not lie. The "shadow fleet" is laughing all the way to the bank while the EU continues to rely on symbolic gestures.
What became of the big announcements? The price cap? The lists of ships? The threats against third countries? It has all come to nothing. Russia is happily selling to India and China, making a fortune from high prices (thanks in part to geopolitical developments in the Middle East), and using the proceeds to fund its war.

Grand words, zero impact. Every new round was touted as "decisive" and a "heavy blow." The result after 21 packages: record exports. The EU has managed to make Russia richer instead of weakening it.
•  Shadow fleet? Hundreds of ships "sanctioned"—yet the oil keeps flowing unchecked. Insurers, shipowners, and buyers in Asia couldn't care less about Brussels' paperwork. The price cap is a joke that no one takes seriously.

The only right course of action.
Anyone seriously wanting to stop the war must detain Russian ships, confiscate the cargo, and sell it—with the proceeds going to Ukraine. Period. Russia does not abide by any rules of international law.
That is when the "we are the good guys and always play by the rules" attitude needs to end.

result.text:
Interesting question. Earlier, I would have said that Ukrainian liberals want to make Ukraine a "normal" country within the West-centred international order. The problem is that this order no longer exists. Ironically, and also tragically, the more Ukraine's integration into this order proceeded, the more it contributed to that order's destruction.

The radical nationalists want a strong, sovereign Ukraine — not in opposition to "the West" in general, but certainly in opposition to its left-liberal tendencies, and ideally in the vanguard of a "white" civilisational revival, possibly in confederation with conservative countries in the region. The idea of an "Intermarium" has been seriously entertained. The problem is that there is no longer any local socio-economic basis for such a Ukraine. And in any "Intermarium" configuration, Ukraine could only be its poorest periphery — and the current conflicts with Poles over history or with Hungary over Hungarian minority rights would look like flowers by comparison.

The realistic future scenarios lie somewhere between something-like-Israel, something-like-Georgia, and something-like-Syria. And the actual trajectory will depend far less on what people inside Ukraine — liberals and radical nationalists included — want, than on the processes unfolding in Russia, the EU, the US, and China.

result.text:
Nearly half (47%) of China’s entire non-government workforce is now trapped in the gig economy, a staggering mathematical reality that redefines the country's economic crisis.

While the daily grind of this "flexible" workforce is clear, official data from the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) and state-linked research exposes the true structural gravity of the situation. Out of China's roughly 730 million total employed citizens, only about 50 million secure stable, coveted public sector and government roles. This leaves a remaining private workforce of 680 million people left to navigate the open market.

When you overlay the China New Employment Forms Research Center's projection that the gig economy will swell to 320 million people this year, the math becomes undeniable. This is no longer just a temporary safety net for displaced workers. It means nearly half of China's entire independent labor force has been systematically locked into precarious survival mode.

With official data proving that almost half of China's private workforce is now confined to the gig economy, do you think this structural shift can permanently absorb the economic slowdown, or is it building a dangerous social and demographic time bomb?

#ChinaEconomy #GigEconomy #FlexibleEmployment #LaborMarket #EconomicCrisis #ChinaJobs

result.text:
A staggering 300 million people are now flooding China's gig economy as the traditional white-collar dream collapses.

For the sixth consecutive year, Chinese blue-collar wage growth has outpaced white-collar pay, steadily narrowing the monthly income gap between the two groups. High-demand roles like maternity caregivers, food delivery riders, and truck drivers are now regularly out-earning desk-bound office professionals. At the center of this structural shift is the explosive growth of the country's "flexible workforce," a group projected to hit 320 million this year, capturing more than 40 percent of all urban employment.

In China, a flexible workforce refers to non-traditional, non-standard employment primarily rooted in the gig and platform economy. Instead of holding a stable, full-time position with a single employer and standard corporate benefits, these individuals work irregular hours as delivery couriers, ride-share drivers, or freelancers. They handle their own social insurance and navigate a system that offers basic immediate security but highly limited long-term development opportunities.

While official reports frame this trend as a dynamic labor market, a wave of skepticism is erupting on platforms like X. Many citizens are openly calling out "flexible employment" as a state euphemism for widespread underemployment and disguised unemployment driven by a brutal economic slowdown. Desperate professionals are flooding delivery and rideshare networks just to stay afloat, which has ironically started driving down actual gig wages due to extreme oversupply.

The underlying data exposes a massive long-term crisis: only 42.3 percent of these flexible workers have confidence in their retirement planning, and barely half see any real opportunity for career advancement. As economic anxiety mounts, the instability of this non-traditional workforce is directly fueling broader social issues, including plunging marriage registrations and reduced social security coverage. By leaning on gig work as a temporary economic shock absorber, the system is accumulating massive long-term risk, trading a stable middle class for a highly vulnerable, hyper-stressed working class.

#ChinaEconomy #GigEconomy #BlueCollarPay #LaborMarket #ChinaWorkforce #EconomicAnxiety

Enough is enough.

At what point do we stop pretending this is normal?

At what point do we stop allowing @realDonaldTrump to insult women, berate women, threaten women, degrade women, and then hide behind power like he is untouchable?

He has been doing this his entire life.

In business.

In politics.

In the courts.

In the Epstein circle.

And now, as president of the United States, he uses the most powerful platform in the world to attack women who speak out, women who stand up, women who dare to tell the truth.

Look at what he is doing to E. Jean Carroll. A jury found him liable for sexually abusing and defaming her — and instead of accountability, he keeps attacking. He keeps trying to intimidate. He keeps using power as a weapon.

And that is exactly the point.

This is not just about insults. This is about control.

He takes away women’s rights.

He attacks women’s credibility.

He covers up the Epstein files.

He protects the powerful men while attacking the women who survived them.

He wants women silent. He wants survivors scared. He wants the country numb.

I’m asking a simple question:

When is enough finally enough?

When do we stand together and say no more?

No more letting the media normalize it.

No more letting politicians excuse it.

No more letting powerful men abuse women and then call themselves victims.

No more silence.

I am calling on this community — women, men, survivors, parents, citizens, everyone with a conscience — to stand together as one voice.

Protect women.

Believe survivors.

Demand the Epstein files.

Hold Trump accountable.

And stop supporting any media outlet, politician, or platform that continues to sanitize, excuse, or cover for this madness.

Because this is not politics anymore.

This is about basic decency.

This is about right and wrong.

This is about whether we are still willing to stand up for the women of this country against a man who has spent his life degrading them.

Enough is enough.

It is time to stand up.
It is time to speak out.
It is time to fight back together.
